comment_text.sn5.5:1.2: Regarded as the greatest in psychic power (AN 1.237:1.1), and, with Khemā, the ideal nun (AN 2.131:1.2, AN 4.176:2.2). She taught other nuns (Thig 13.5:27.3), and was devoted to the Buddha (Bu NP 5:1.1.1).
comment_text.sn5.5:2.1: These verses are included in Uppalavaṇṇā’s at Thig 11.1:7.1, with some differences.
comment_text.sn5.5:2.3: Literally, “There is no-one to whom you are second in the element of beauty” (na catthi te dutiyā vaṇṇadhātu,). Thig 11.1:7.3 reads Na cāpi te dutiyo atthi koci, “There is no male companion with you”. The two lines use dutiya (“second”) in different senses, as the ordinal number, and as “companion”. The Therīgāthā commentary offers two explanations, the second of which appears to lean on a version of the line similar to that found here. This, together with the fact that the unusual term vaṇṇadhātu seems like a direct reference to her name, suggests that this version may be the original one.
comment_text.sn5.5:6.4: Despite her dauntless rejection of Māra here, a story in the Vinaya says she was once raped by a Vedic student who was infatuated with her (Bu Pj 1:10.5.1). The Buddha declared that one who does not consent is innocent. It seems hard to reconcile the two events, unless the Vinaya incident was when she was young and not yet accomplished. If that were the case, perhaps Māra is deliberately capitalizing on the past assault, assuming she will be traumatized by it.

root_text.sn5.5:1.2: Atha kho uppalavaṇṇā bhikkhunī pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā …pe…
root_text.sn5.5:1.4: Atha kho māro pāpimā uppalavaṇṇāya bhikkhuniyā bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo samādhimhā cāvetukāmo yena uppalavaṇṇā bhikkhunī tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā uppalavaṇṇaṁ bhikkhuniṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn5.5:3.2: “ko nu khvāyaṁ manusso vā amanusso vā gāthaṁ bhāsatī”ti?
root_text.sn5.5:3.4: “māro kho ayaṁ pāpimā mama bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo samādhimhā cāvetukāmo gāthaṁ bhāsatī”ti.
root_text.sn5.5:3.5: Atha kho uppalavaṇṇā bhikkhunī “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāhi paccabhāsi:
root_text.sn5.5:7.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ uppalavaṇṇā bhikkhunī”ti dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn5.5:1.2: Then the nun Uppalavaṇṇā robed up in the morning …
translation_text.sn5.5:1.3: and stood at the root of a sal tree in full flower.
translation_text.sn5.5:1.4: Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Uppalavaṇṇā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse:
translation_text.sn5.5:2.1: “You’ve come to this sal tree all crowned with flowers,
translation_text.sn5.5:2.2: and stand at its root all alone, O nun.
translation_text.sn5.5:2.3: Your beauty is second to none—
translation_text.sn5.5:2.4: silly girl, aren’t you afraid of rascals?”
translation_text.sn5.5:3.2: “Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?”
translation_text.sn5.5:3.4: “This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!”
translation_text.sn5.5:3.5: Then Uppalavaṇṇā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse:
translation_text.sn5.5:4.3: I’d stir not a hair nor panic.
translation_text.sn5.5:4.4: I’m not scared of you, Māra, even alone.
translation_text.sn5.5:5.3: I could stand between your eyebrows
translation_text.sn5.5:5.4: and you still wouldn’t see me.
translation_text.sn5.5:6.1: I’m the master of my own mind,
translation_text.sn5.5:6.2: I’ve developed the bases of psychic power well.
translation_text.sn5.5:6.4: and I’m not afraid of you, sir!”
translation_text.sn5.5:7.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Uppalavaṇṇā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.

reference_text.sn5.5:3.1: csp1ed12.133, csp2ed12.133, ms12S1_967, ndp12.132, pts-vp-pli1ed1.132, pts-vp-pli2ed1.289
